summary:
The Mongol conquest of China (1211–1279 AD) was a sixty-eight-year campaign involving multiple phases against three separate Chinese states — the Jin Dynasty of northern China (conquered 1234), the Western Xia (conquered 1227), and the [[event:3044|Southern Song Dynasty]] (conquered 1279) — representing one of the most prolonged and costly military campaigns in the [[event:91|Mongol Empire's]] history. China's walled cities, canals, and rice paddies were fundamentally hostile terrain for steppe cavalry, forcing the Mongols to develop sophisticated siege warfare, naval capabilities, and administrative techniques far beyond their nomadic origins.

The conquest was devastating in demographic terms. Northern China under the Jin Dynasty lost an estimated 35–50% of its population to Mongol warfare, famine, and disease — from roughly 50 million people to perhaps 30 million. Cities including Beijing, Kaifeng, and Zhongdu were comprehensively destroyed. Yet the Mongol conquerors also adapted pragmatically to Chinese civilization: they employed Chinese engineers, administrators, and scholars; adopted Chinese administrative techniques; and [[event:2229|Kublai Khan]] completed the conquest of the Southern Song while simultaneously constructing a Chinese-style imperial court, taking the Chinese dynastic name 'Yuan,' and patronizing Chinese culture, art, and scholarship. The Yuan Dynasty (1271–1368) represented a unique synthesis of Mongol political power with Chinese civilization, creating the conditions for [[event:2230|Marco Polo's]] famous visit and the Eurasian connectivity of the Pax Mongolica.
